contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Choose hashing when the decisive signal is equality-based membership or frequency state; choose sorting when the result or later reasoning requires ordered adjacency, ranges, or deterministic traversal.

items.prompt: A stream must report whether each arriving identifier has been seen before; result order follows arrival order and no sorted output is required. Which primary signal is strongest?

options.text: Equality-based membership state favors a hash set.
Sorting is required because duplicate detection always needs adjacency.
Compare only consecutive arrivals.
Binary-search the unsorted arrivals.

feedback.reason: The decisive operation is repeated equality-based membership lookup.
feedback.details: A hash set directly represents seen identifiers and can preserve arrival-order processing without first imposing an ordering.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_all: Sorting can expose adjacent duplicates, but online seen-before lookup does not require order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_neighbors: Equal identifiers may be separated by many other values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_search_unsorted: Binary search lacks a sorted search domain.

items.prompt: A report must list all distinct values in ascending order and combine equal values into adjacent groups. Which signal favors sorting?

options.text: The output requires deterministic value order and equal-run adjacency.
Hash lookup is expected O(1), so ordering requirements can be ignored.
The implementation should contain one loop.
Only first-seen membership matters.

feedback.reason: Sorting directly creates the adjacency and order consumed by the result.
feedback.details: A hash map could count values, but a later key sort would reintroduce ordering cost and should be included in the strategy comparison.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_expected: A hash table does not by itself produce ascending groups.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_pass_name: Loop count does not establish the required output order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_seen_only: The contract explicitly requires ascending grouped output.

items.prompt: Keys support equality and hashing but have no meaningful total order. The task asks only whether a queried key exists. Which strategy signal is valid?

options.text: Hash lookup matches the equality contract without inventing an order.
Sorting is mandatory for every lookup problem.
Equal-run grouping is the primary requirement.
Sort by memory address to create a semantic order.

feedback.reason: Hashing requires equality and a compatible hash function, whereas sorting requires an ordering relation.
feedback.details: When only membership is needed and no legal total order exists, the lookup signal clearly favors hashing.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_required: Sorting requires a comparator or total order that the contract does not provide.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_order: An incidental address order does not satisfy a domain contract.

items.prompt: Records are comparable by timestamp, and the query asks for every record in a time interval in chronological order. Which signal is primary?

options.text: Ordered range traversal favors sorting or an ordered representation.
Hash equality lookup is sufficient.
Only frequency counting is relevant.
The task is duplicate detection.

feedback.reason: Range queries depend on relative order, not only equality.
feedback.details: Sorting establishes the boundary and traversal order needed to emit the interval.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_equality: Equality lookup does not directly locate all values inside an interval.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_only: The result requires a range and chronological order.

items.prompt: A one-pass job must count occurrences of each token, and the caller accepts groups in arbitrary order. Which primary signal favors hashing?

options.text: Direct key-to-count updates favor a hash map.
Sort because every frequency table must be ordered.
Count only adjacent equal tokens.
Reorder the original token sequence.

feedback.reason: The required state is an equality-keyed count per token.
feedback.details: Without an ordered-output requirement, hashing avoids imposing an order that the contract does not consume.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_for_order: The result order is explicitly arbitrary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mutate_input: Mutation is unnecessary for the stated result.

items.prompt: A task asks for the closest pair of numeric values, not their original positions. Which signal favors sorting over a hash table?

options.text: After sorting, the minimum difference must occur between adjacent values.
Exact membership is the only required relation.
Expected constant-time hash operations prove optimality.
The task is only to detect duplicates.

feedback.reason: Sorting creates a structural adjacency invariant for the distance objective.
feedback.details: A hash table represents equality well but not nearest ordered neighbors.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.membership_only: Closest-pair reasoning depends on order and distance.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_constant: A hash table does not expose neighboring values by magnitude.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_only: Duplicates are one zero-distance case, but the full task includes nonzero distances.

items.prompt: Input is already sorted, and the task is to output value-frequency groups in that same order. Which strategy is most direct?

options.text: Scan consecutive equal runs without building a hash table.
Build a hash map because hashing is always faster than sorting.
Sort the already sorted input again.
Perform random membership queries for every value.

feedback.reason: The existing order already supplies duplicate adjacency and deterministic group order.
feedback.details: A linear run scan consumes that structure directly; expected hash lookup offers no asymptotic advantage and uses extra memory.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rehash_all: The ordering work is already done and a run scan is linear with constant auxiliary state.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_lookup: That adds unnecessary work and state.

items.prompt: A task must return the original index of the first occurrence of each distinct value while preserving first-seen order. Which signal favors a hash-based design?

options.text: Store first indices in a hash map while scanning the original sequence.
Sort values alone and infer original indices afterward.
Only sorted adjacency matters.
Binary-search the unsorted original sequence.

feedback.reason: The required relation is equality lookup coupled with original-position metadata.
feedback.details: A hash map can retain the first index while the scan preserves first-seen order.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_values_only: Sorting values without index metadata loses the required identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.adjacent_groups: The contract prioritizes first-seen order and original indices.

items.prompt: Objects are comparable by a canonical key but are expensive to hash. The result needs one deterministic ordered traversal and no later equality queries. Which signal favors sorting?

options.text: The result consumes total order directly, while hashing would add unused lookup state.
The presence of keys implies a hash map.
Expected linear build cost dominates every other contract.
Any one-pass implementation is automatically preferable.

feedback.reason: The strategy should serve the relation the result consumes.
feedback.details: When order is the output and hashing is expensive, sorting can be the more natural and possibly faster end-to-end choice.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_word: Keys can support ordering without requiring hashing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.expected_linear: Hash cost and unused state must be included.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_pass: The output itself requires deterministic ordering.

items.prompt: A problem statement contains the word "lookup," but the actual query asks for the smallest key not less than x. What should drive the choice?

options.text: The successor-order relation, which favors sorting or an ordered structure.
The word "lookup," which proves hashing.
Any hash table because expected lookup is constant.
Variable names such as key and table.

feedback.reason: The semantic query is an ordered boundary search, not equality membership.
feedback.details: Strategy classification must follow the relation required by the answer.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.keyword_lookup: Keywords do not define the required relation.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_any: A plain hash table does not locate the next ordered key.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_names: Names are not a strategy invariant.

items.prompt: A problem requires both exact membership queries and final ascending output. What is the correct initial conclusion?

options.text: Both lookup and ordering signals exist; compare the full workload or use a hybrid design.
Hashing automatically satisfies ascending output.
Sorting makes every exact query expected O(1).
Choose only from query cost and ignore final output.

feedback.reason: Mixed contracts can justify sorting, hashing plus key sorting, or maintaining both representations.
feedback.details: The choice depends on query volume, memory, updates, and whether the ordered result can reuse preprocessing.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_always: Hash iteration order is not a general ascending-order contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_always: Binary search on sorted data is logarithmic, not expected constant time.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_output: The final ordering requirement is part of correctness.

items.prompt: Which result contracts primarily require equality-keyed state? Select all that apply.

options.text: Repeated equality-based membership tests.
Incremental counts by key with arbitrary output order.
Retaining first-seen indices while scanning original order.
Finding the next greater key and emitting an ordered range.

feedback.reason: Hashing is strongest when the state is keyed by equality and does not require ordered traversal.
feedback.details: Ordered successor and range semantics point toward sorting or an ordered structure.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.range_successor: These require ordering rather than equality alone.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.frequency: A map directly updates key-associated state.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.first_index: A map can associate each key with its first position.

items.prompt: Which result contracts primarily consume relative order or adjacency? Select all that apply.

options.text: Deterministic ascending output.
Grouping equal values into consecutive runs.
Reporting values in an ordered interval.
Only arbitrary-order exact membership on unorderable keys.

feedback.reason: Sorting is valuable when relative order becomes reusable structure.
feedback.details: Pure equality membership does not consume that structure.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.range_query: Ordered boundaries and traversal are central.

items.prompt: Which questions should be answered before choosing hashing or sorting? Select all that apply.

options.text: Does the result require equality lookup, relative order, or both?
Are keys hashable, comparable, or both?
Must original order, deterministic group order, or indices be preserved?
Is a variable named map or sorted?

feedback.reason: The decision follows semantic operations and observable contracts.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_name: Names do not establish a legal strategy.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.relation: The required relation is the primary boundary.

items.prompt: Which situations can make both hashing and sorting correct candidates? Select all that apply.

options.text: Determine whether any duplicate exists in a reorderable array.
Count values and then emit keys in ascending order.
Serve many membership queries and later produce ordered output.
Keys support neither hashing nor a comparison contract.

feedback.reason: Correctness can leave multiple strategies available.
feedback.details: The final choice then depends on workload, memory, mutation, guarantees, and constants.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unhashable_unorderable: Neither standard strategy is directly legal.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_exists: A set or sort-and-scan can both be correct.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_ordered: Sort-and-run or hash-then-sort-keys can both work.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.many_membership_final_order: A hybrid or either preprocessing path may be reasonable.

items.prompt: Which observations are insufficient by themselves to choose a hash map? Select all that apply.

options.text: The prompt uses the word "lookup."
A hash operation is expected O(1).
The input contains duplicates.
The task requires only equality membership and keys are hashable.

feedback.reason: Surface vocabulary and isolated operation costs do not determine the strategy.
feedback.details: A legal equality-only contract is a real mechanism signal.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.equality_only: This is a substantive hash signal.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.word_lookup: The lookup may be ordered rather than equality-based.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.expected_o1: Full workload and correctness constraints remain.

items.prompt: Arrange the decision process for distinguishing a lookup signal from an ordering signal.

elements.text: State the exact observable result and query relation.
Check whether legal equality/hash and total-order operations exist.
Identify whether membership state or ordered adjacency/ranges will be reused.
Compare memory, mutation, guarantees, and workload before selecting the strategy.

feedback.reason: The strategy follows from the relation and reusable structure required by the contract.
feedback.details: Cost comparison is meaningful only after legal candidates have been identified.

items.prompt: An array of n hashable keys receives q exact membership queries. Compare building a hash set once with sorting once and using binary search, assuming expected O(1) hash operations and in-place comparison sorting.
constraints: Hash-set build uses O(n) auxiliary entries.
In-place comparison sort uses O(log n) stack space.

sort_time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

sort_time.sort_and_binary_queries: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: The lookup signal favors expected hash time, while sorting offers an ordered representation and lower stated auxiliary memory under these assumptions.
feedback.details: This calculation does not make hashing universally preferable; output order, mutation, guarantees, and key-operation costs can change the decision.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Choose hashing when the decisive signal is equality-based membership or frequency state; choose sorting when the result or later reasoning requires ordered adjacency, ranges, or deterministic traversal.
